applied_optimizations.name: Triton kernel with BLOCK_ROWS=256
applied_optimizations.description: Triton RMSNorm kernel with tensor permutation for contiguous memory access. Each program handles 256 rows to amortize launch overhead (115K programs vs 29M). Correct output but 3.7x slower than PyTorch due to remaining launch overhead and loop costs.

attempted_but_failed.name: 1D grid with 29M programs
attempted_but_failed.reason: Integer overflow in pid decoding on AMD Triton - later batch positions produced incorrect output

attempted_but_failed.name: 2D grid approaches
attempted_but_failed.reason: HIP error: invalid argument - grid dimension exceeded limits

attempted_but_failed.name: Manual loop unrolling
attempted_but_failed.reason: Cannot access tensor shape in kernel for bounds checking

attempted_but_failed.name: torch.compile
attempted_but_failed.reason: Achieved 1.66x speedup but task requires Triton @triton.jit kernels specifically

notes: PyTorch reference: 7.02ms (very efficient for simple elementwise ops)
Key challenge: 29M positions requires either massive parallelism or amortization
BLOCK_ROWS=256 reduces launches to 115K but loop overhead is significant
Memory access is now optimal (contiguous) after permuting tensor
For simple elementwise ops on AMD, PyTorch's fused kernels are hard to beat with Triton

global_notes: Stage 1 complete: Established baseline and profile for Triton implementation
Triton kernel is correct but cannot beat PyTorch baseline for this simple elementwise operation
The fundamental issue is that RMSNorm on this shape has too many small independent computations
Further optimization would need: larger BLOCK_ROWS, or a completely different algorithmic approach
